spec_match.rationale: Calls `Iterator.concat()` directly across the iterable object types the TC39 proposal targets - arrays, Sets, Map.values(), String objects, generators - and a side-by-side eager-spread comparison that surfaces the allocation difference the spec is designed to avoid.

interactive.rationale: Six preset buttons run real `Iterator.concat()` calls with output logging, including a thrown-error case, plus a paired eager-vs-lazy split panel with separate run buttons.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises arrays, Sets, generators, and Map entries; the page delivers all four plus a String-object iterable example and an iterator-helpers chain.

edge_cases.rationale: Polyfill fallback for older browsers is implemented and the support banner flips accordingly. The page now demonstrates mid-sequence error propagation, shows that later sources are not pulled after the throw, and explicitly calls out the sync-only `Symbol.iterator` contract for async iterables and `ReadableStream` sources.

design_system.rationale: Variables drive most styling, but inline `style="font-size:0.78rem;background:var(--bg-stone)..."` on the eager/lazy compare panes is bulky and would be cleaner as a class — minor consistency issue. Buttons follow the global rule.

openQuestions.title: Measure heap allocation difference
openQuestions.detail: The eager-vs-lazy comparison shows the conceptual difference but doesn't quantify it. Add `performance.memory.usedJSHeapSize` deltas (with a feature-detection caveat) to make the savings concrete on large-iterable runs.

summary: Comprehensive demonstrator covering the iterable object types the spec targets, with a working polyfill fallback, error propagation, and an explicit sync-only callout. Verified in Chrome Canary 151 with chrome-devtools-mcp by exercising every button, including the throw case; the remaining follow-up is a heap-allocation deltabar.
